---
title: "Responsible Use of AI in Research"
author: Trey Saddler
date: 2024-12-01
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    preview-links: auto
    server:
      port: 4200
---

## About Me

```{r}
library(timevis)

data <- data.frame(
  id      = 1:4,
  content = c("Salish Kootenai College", "NIEHS Postbac",
              "Office of Data Science", "Predictive Toxicology Branch"),
  start   = c("2010-08-01", "2015-08-01", "2018-06-01", "2021-08-01"),
  end     = c("2015-06-09", "2018-06-01", "2021-08-01", "2024-12-02")
)

timevis(data)
```

::: {.notes}
Hello everyone. I'd like to start by introducing myself and sharing a bit about my background. I graduated from Salish Kootenai College, a tribal college in Pablo, MT, in 2015. I received my bachelors of Life Science with a focus in environmental health. After I graduated, I joined the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences as a postbac. I started working in wet lab for a year but I learned I'm not great with taking care of cells. So I started working with Dr. Auerbach for two years analyzing data using R and Shiny. Following that, I spent three years as a data scientist contractor in the NIEHS Office of Data Science. After a reorganization, I've been in the same role with the NIEHS Predictive Toxicology Branch for the last 3 years.
:::

## Previous work

- Manage Posit Teams deployment for NIEHS
- Admin for NIEHS GitHub
- R Shiny applications for analyzing data

::: {.notes}
One of my primary responsibilites for the past few years has been managing our Posit Teams deployment for NIEHS, which has given me insight into collaborative data science environments. I also administer the NIEHS GitHub Organization. Additionally, I've developed R Shiny applications for data analysis, which has honed my skills in creating interactive tools for researchers.
:::

## Large Language Model (LLM) Work

::: {.incremental}
- Started playing with ChatGPT in January 2023
- Initiate ToxPipe project in May 2023
- Continued to expand our LLM capabilities
:::

::: {.notes}
My journey into the world of Large Language Models, or LLMs, began in January 2023 when I started experimenting with ChatGPT. At first I thought that ChatGPT was just an interesting toy. After watching some YouTube videos on how other people were using it, I quickly saw how it could be applied to our work at NIEHS. This led to the initiation of the ToxPipe project in May 2023, where we began exploring how LLMs could enhance our toxicology research. Since then, I've been actively involved in expanding our LLM capabilities, constantly seeking new ways to integrate these powerful tools into our research processes. This hands-on experience with LLMs in a research setting is what brings me here today to discuss the responsible use of AI in research.
:::

## Agenda

::: {.incremental}

```{=html}
<iframe width="1080" height="500" src="html/osp_od_nih_gov-policies-artificial-intelligence-2024-12-01.html" title="Chatbots have thouroughly infiltrated scientific publishing"></iframe>
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Let's begin with a brief overview of AI and its profound impact on scientific research. 

This brings me to the importance of ethical considerations in AI use. As we integrate these powerful tools into our research, we must be mindful of the ethical implications. Today, we'll focus specifically on Large Language Models, or LLMs, and their potential in research. These models represent some of the most advanced AI technologies available to researchers today, and understanding how to use them responsibly is key to harnessing their full potential.
:::

## Understanding Large Language Models (LLMs)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Definition and basic functionality
- Examples of popular LLMs
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Current and potential applications in research
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Let's take a moment to understand what Large Language Models are and how they function. In simple terms, LLMs are AI systems trained on vast amounts of text data, enabling them to understand and generate human-like text. Some popular examples include GPT-3, BERT, and T5. These models have found numerous applications in research already. They're being used for everything from literature reviews and data analysis to generating research hypotheses and assisting in scientific writing. The potential applications are expanding rapidly, and we're only beginning to scratch the surface of what's possible. As we delve deeper into LLMs, it's important to consider both their capabilities and limitations in a research context.
:::

## Benefits of LLMs in Research {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}

::: {.text-center}
![](media/github-copilot.svg)

Coding assistant
:::

- Accelerating literature reviews and data analysis
- Generating hypotheses and research questions
- Assisting in writing and editing scientific papers
- Extract information from historical documentation

:::

::: {.notes}
Now, let's explore the benefits of LLMs in research. One of the most well-known applications is GitHub Copilot, an AI coding assistant that can significantly speed up the coding process. But the benefits extend far beyond coding. LLMs are proving invaluable in accelerating literature reviews and data analysis, tasks that traditionally consume a significant portion of a researcher's time. They're also being used to generate hypotheses and research questions, offering new perspectives that researchers might not have considered. In the writing process, LLMs can assist in drafting and editing scientific papers, helping researchers articulate their ideas more clearly. Additionally, these models are powerful tools for extracting information from historical documentation, making it easier to build on past research. As we discuss these benefits, it's important to remember that LLMs are tools to enhance, not replace, human researchers. The goal is to use these tools to augment our capabilities and push the boundaries of what's possible in research.
:::

## Ethical Concerns and Challenges

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Bias and Fairness

- Training data biases
- Amplifying existing biases

### Transparency

["Black box" nature of LLMs](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-08141-1)
![](media/screenshots/open-ai-systems-closed.png)
- Understanding decision-making processes

### Privacy

- Handling sensitive research data
- Ensuring participant confidentiality

### IP and Attribution

- Determining authorship and contribution
- Citing AI-generated content

### Reliability

- Variability in LLM outputs
- Ensuring consistent results
:::

::: {.notes}
While the benefits of LLMs in research are significant, we must also address the ethical concerns and challenges they present. Let's break these down into several key areas. First, there's the issue of bias and fairness. LLMs can inherit and even amplify biases present in their training data, which can lead to skewed results in research. Transparency is another major concern. The "black box" nature of these models makes it difficult to understand their decision-making processes, which is crucial in scientific research. Privacy is a critical issue, especially when handling sensitive research data or ensuring participant confidentiality. We must be vigilant about how we use LLMs with such data. Intellectual property and attribution pose unique challenges in the age of AI. How do we determine authorship and contribution when AI is involved in the research process? How should we cite AI-generated content? Finally, there's the question of reliability. LLM outputs can be variable, and ensuring consistent results is vital for scientific integrity. As we navigate these challenges, it's crucial that we develop robust guidelines and best practices to ensure the responsible use of AI in research.
:::

## Best Practices for Responsible AI Use in Research

::: {.incremental}
- [NIH AI Policies](https://osp.od.nih.gov/policies/artificial-intelligence#tab0/)
- Ensure transparency in AI-assisted research processes
- Implement rigorous validation and peer review processes
- Provide proper training and education on AI ethics
:::

::: {.notes}
/var/folders/2p/6tgtqdr57vz3bvkppl1g1qy8_rv9z_/T/images/Snagit 2021 2024-12-02 12.07.46.png
:::

## Developing Clear Guidelines

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Establish institutional policies
- Define acceptable uses of AI in research
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Create protocols for AI integration
- Set standards for documentation and reporting
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Developing clear guidelines is crucial for the responsible use of AI in research. At an institutional level, we need to establish comprehensive policies that govern the use of AI tools. These policies should clearly define what constitutes acceptable use of AI in various research contexts. It's not enough to have general guidelines; we need specific protocols for integrating AI into different stages of the research process. This might include steps for validating AI-generated hypotheses or analyzing AI-assisted data interpretations. Additionally, we must set rigorous standards for documentation and reporting. Researchers should be required to provide detailed information about the AI tools used, including model specifications, training data, and any limitations or potential biases. By creating and enforcing these guidelines, we can ensure that AI is used in a way that enhances rather than compromises the quality and integrity of scientific research.
:::

## Ensuring Transparency

::: {.incremental}
- Clearly disclose AI use in research methods
- Document specifics of AI models and parameters used
- Provide access to AI-generated data and analyses when possible
- Explain the role of AI in forming conclusions
:::

::: {.notes}
Transparency is paramount when using AI in research. We must start by clearly disclosing the use of AI in our research methods. This isn't just a footnote; it should be an integral part of our methodology sections. We need to go beyond simply mentioning AI use and document the specifics of the AI models and parameters used. This includes details about the model architecture, training data, and any fine-tuning processes. Whenever possible, we should provide access to AI-generated data and analyses. This allows other researchers to verify and build upon our work. It's also crucial to explain the role of AI in forming our conclusions. We need to be clear about which parts of our research were AI-assisted and how AI insights were integrated with human expertise. By ensuring this level of transparency, we not only maintain the integrity of our research but also contribute to the broader scientific community's understanding of AI's capabilities and limitations in research contexts.
:::

## Implementing Rigorous Validation

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Human Oversight

- Establish human review processes
- Combine AI insights with expert knowledge

### Peer Review

- Develop guidelines for reviewing AI-assisted research
- Train reviewers on AI-specific considerations

### Reproducibility

- Ensure AI-assisted results can be replicated
- Provide detailed methodologies for AI use
:::

::: {.notes}
Implementing rigorous validation processes is crucial when using AI in research. Let's break this down into three key areas. First, human oversight is essential. We need to establish robust human review processes for AI-generated outputs. This isn't about doubting AI, but about combining AI insights with expert knowledge to ensure the best possible outcomes. Second, we need to adapt our peer review processes. This means developing specific guidelines for reviewing AI-assisted research and training reviewers on AI-specific considerations. Reviewers need to understand how to evaluate the use of AI tools and interpret AI-assisted results. Finally, we must ensure reproducibility. AI-assisted results should be replicable by other researchers. This requires providing detailed methodologies for AI use, including model specifications, parameters, and any data preprocessing steps. By implementing these validation processes, we can maintain the rigor and reliability of our research in the age of AI.
:::

## Training and Education

::: {.incremental}
- Develop AI ethics courses for researchers
- Provide ongoing training on latest AI developments
- Foster interdisciplinary collaboration on AI ethics
- Encourage critical thinking about AI applications
:::

::: {.notes}
Training and education are foundational to the responsible use of AI in research. We need to develop comprehensive AI ethics courses for researchers at all levels. These courses should cover not just the technical aspects of AI, but also the ethical implications and best practices for responsible use. Given how rapidly AI technology is evolving, we must provide ongoing training to keep researchers updated on the latest developments. This isn't just about learning new tools, but understanding their potential impacts on research processes and outcomes. We should also foster interdisciplinary collaboration on AI ethics. By bringing together experts from fields like computer science, ethics, and various research disciplines, we can develop more robust and nuanced approaches to AI use in research. Finally, we need to encourage critical thinking about AI applications. Researchers should be trained to question and evaluate AI tools, understanding their strengths and limitations. By prioritizing this kind of education and training, we can create a research community that's well-equipped to harness the power of AI responsibly and ethically.
:::

## Case Studies {.smaller}

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Success Story

- Example of responsible AI use in research
- Highlight best practices implemented
- Discuss positive outcomes and lessons learned

### Cautionary Tale

- Example of problematic AI use in research
- Identify ethical issues and missteps
- Discuss consequences and preventive measures
:::

::: {.notes}
Let's look at two case studies that illustrate the importance of responsible AI use in research. First, a success story. In this case, researchers used an LLM to assist in analyzing a large corpus of medical literature on a rare disease. They implemented best practices by clearly documenting the AI model used, its training data, and how it was integrated into their research process. They combined the AI's ability to quickly process vast amounts of text with human expert review of the AI-generated insights. This approach led to the identification of previously overlooked connections between symptoms, accelerating the research significantly. The key lesson here is that when used responsibly and transparently, AI can be a powerful tool to enhance human capabilities in research.

Now, let's consider a cautionary tale. In this instance, researchers relied heavily on an AI model to generate hypotheses about gene interactions without fully understanding the model's limitations or potential biases in its training data. They didn't implement rigorous validation processes or clearly disclose the extent of AI use in their published work. This led to the publication of misleading conclusions that had to be retracted when other researchers couldn't replicate the results. The consequences were significant, damaging the researchers' credibility and wasting valuable resources. This case underscores the importance of transparency, validation, and a thorough understanding of AI tools in research. It also highlights the need for clear guidelines and peer review processes specifically designed for AI-assisted research. By learning from both these success stories and cautionary tales, we can better navigate the integration of AI into our research practices.
:::

## Future Directions

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Evolving AI technologies in research
- Anticipated challenges and opportunities
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- The role of policy and regulation
- Importance of ongoing ethical discussions
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
As we look to the future, it's clear that AI technologies in research will continue to evolve at a rapid pace. We're likely to see more sophisticated LLMs, as well as AI tools tailored specifically for scientific research tasks. This evolution will bring both exciting opportunities and new challenges. We might see AI assisting in more complex aspects of the research process, from experimental design to data interpretation. However, as these tools become more powerful, the ethical considerations will become even more critical. We'll need to stay vigilant about issues like bias, transparency, and the potential for over-reliance on AI.

The role of policy and regulation will be crucial in shaping the future of AI in research. We'll likely see more comprehensive guidelines from institutions and governing bodies about AI use in scientific studies. These policies will need to balance encouraging innovation with ensuring ethical and responsible use. It's also vital that we maintain ongoing ethical discussions within the scientific community. As AI capabilities expand, new ethical questions will arise, and we need to be prepared to address them. This might involve regular conferences, workshops, or forums dedicated to AI ethics in research. By staying ahead of these issues, we can ensure that AI remains a powerful tool for advancing scientific knowledge while upholding the highest ethical standards.
:::

## Conclusion

::: {.incremental}
- Recap of key points
- Emphasize the importance of responsible AI use
- Call to action for researchers and institutions
:::

::: {.notes}
As we conclude, let's recap the key points we've discussed today. We've explored the tremendous potential of AI, particularly Large Language Models
:::
